planting.note: Plant dormant canes or container plants in spring; in zone 9 plant February–April. Prime-Ark 45 is a primocane-fruiting thorny erect blackberry that produces a fall primocane crop in August–September and a spring floricane crop in May–June in zone 9. Despite its thorns, the very large, high-quality fruit makes it worth the harvesting inconvenience. Provide sturdy trellis support for the heavy primocanes.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil; Prime-Ark 45 is the premier primocane-fruiting blackberry for the Southeast from the University of Arkansas, producing very large (among the biggest primocane-fruiting blackberries), firm, sweet, intensely flavored berries on both first-year (summer) and second-year (spring) canes. The fruit quality is significantly higher than earlier primocane blackberry releases — larger, firmer, sweeter berries that are excellent for fresh eating and hold well after harvest. Prime-Ark 45 is thorny, unlike the thornless Prime-Jan, but the superior fruit quality compensates for the harvesting difficulty. The number '45' in the name refers to internal University of Arkansas breeding program designation. In zone 9 the fall primocane crop matures in August–September when few other berries are available, providing fresh blackberries in a season gap that no other easily-grown small fruit fills. The spring floricane crop in May–June follows the standard blackberry season timing.
sources: University of Arkansas Division of Agriculture — Prime-Ark 45 Release Notes
University of Georgia Extension — Primocane Blackberry Varieties
North Carolina State University Extension — Prime-Ark 45 Performance

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in fall or early spring; in zone 9 plant October–February. Summit requires approximately 400–500 chill hours — suitable for zone 9a but marginal for warmer zone 9b areas. Cross-pollinate with another mid-range chill southern highbush variety. In zone 9 it ripens in late May–June.

directions: Plant in full sun in very acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), organically rich, well-drained, sandy soil; Summit is a southern highbush blueberry released by the University of Georgia for its outstanding combination of large, flavorful berries and good disease resistance to the humid-summer conditions of the Southeast. The large, firm, dark blue berries have excellent flavor — sweet and complex with good sugar-to-acid balance — and a dry scar that reduces the tendency to leak after picking, improving storage quality. Summit is specifically bred for disease resistance to mummy berry (Monilinia vaccinia-corymbosi) and to stem blight, which are the primary fungal diseases limiting blueberry production in the humid Gulf South. This disease package makes Summit particularly valuable in zone 9 conditions where wet summers favor fungal diseases. The mid-season ripening (late May–June) slots Summit between the earliest varieties and the latest-season types, contributing to an extended home harvest season when planted with Biloxi, Flordablue, and Windsor.
sources: University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Summit Blueberry Release
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Disease-Resistant Blueberry Varieties
North Carolina State University Extension — Summit Southern Highbush Blueberry

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants in spring; in zone 9 plant February–April. Wyldewood produces heavier crops with cross-pollination from another elderberry variety nearby. In zone 9 it produces abundant flowers in May–June and ripe fruit clusters in July–August. Elderberries spread by root suckers in suitable conditions; manage spread with mowing around plantings.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in moist, fertile, well-drained to moderately moist soil; Wyldewood is a named American elderberry (Sambucus canadensis) cultivar developed from wild selections in Oklahoma by the Oklahoma State University extension program, selected for extraordinary production of large, dense berry clusters — corymbs up to 10–14 inches across with hundreds of small, dark purple-black berries per cluster. Wyldewood is considered one of the highest-yielding American elderberry cultivars, with mature plants producing 12–20 pounds or more of fruit clusters annually. The berries are used for elderberry syrup (widely used as an immune-support supplement), elderflower and elderberry liqueurs and wines, jams, pies, and lemonade. Raw elderberries contain very small amounts of cyanogenic compounds and should not be eaten raw in large quantities; cooking neutralizes these compounds. Wyldewood is specifically bred for Oklahoma and surrounding regions and performs excellently throughout zone 9 conditions.
sources: Oklahoma State University Extension — Wyldewood Elderberry Selection
University of Missouri Extension — American Elderberry Cultivars
Penn State Extension — Elderberry Production Guide

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant February–April or October–November. Sweet Scarlet goumi is self-fertile but produces more heavily with another goumi nearby for cross-pollination. In zone 9 it bears fruit earlier (May–June) than in northern climates. The nitrogen-fixing root nodules improve soil fertility over time — a useful attribute in poor soils.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained soil — poor and lean soils are acceptable as the nitrogen-fixing roots improve fertility over time; Sweet Scarlet Goumi is a named cultivar of the Japanese goumi bush (Elaeagnus multiflora), a nitrogen-fixing shrub producing clusters of small, oval, bright scarlet-red berries with silvery speckles — the appearance is immediately distinctive and unlike any common North American fruit. The ripe berries have a tart-sweet, astringent flavor similar to a cranberry crossed with a sour cherry, with resinous notes and a unique aromatic quality. The astringency decreases and sweetness increases as the berries ripen fully in late May–June. Goumi berries are high in lycopene (the red carotenoid), vitamins A and C, and contain higher protein than most berries. They are eaten fresh when fully ripe, made into jams, juices, and wine, or dried. Sweet Scarlet is specifically selected for sweeter fruit than many other goumi selections. As an Elaeagnus species, the shrub fixes nitrogen and is an excellent companion plant near fruit trees in a food forest design.
sources: Oikos Tree Crops — Goumi Berry Cultivar Notes
University of Nebraska Extension — Uncommon Small Fruits
Raintree Nursery — Goumi Berry Growing Guide

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant February–April or October–November. Tillamook is self-fertile but cross-pollination with Sweet Scarlet or another goumi variety improves fruit set. In zone 9 it bears in May–June. The shrub is drought-tolerant once established and thrives in lean soils due to nitrogen fixation by root nodules.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, poor-to-moderately-fertile soil — avoid over-fertilizing as excess nitrogen suppresses fruit production in this nitrogen-fixing shrub; Tillamook is a Raintree Nursery-selected goumi cultivar known for producing larger, sweeter fruit than most other goumi selections, with a berry size approaching that of a small cherry and a flavor noticeably sweeter and more pleasant for fresh eating than the more astringent wild-type goumi. The large, bright scarlet to deep red berries ripen with the distinctive silvery speckle pattern characteristic of Elaeagnus species, and the mature ripe fruit has a sweet-tart flavor with less of the mouth-puckering tannin that makes less-ripe goumi berries challenging to eat raw. Tillamook was selected from plants grown at Raintree Nursery in Morton, Washington — a Pacific Northwest selection that nonetheless performs well in zone 9 given its cold hardiness and drought tolerance. Like all goumi, it fixes nitrogen and improves soil fertility for neighboring plants in a permaculture or food forest context.
sources: Raintree Nursery — Tillamook Goumi Selection Notes
Oikos Tree Crops — Elaeagnus multiflora Named Varieties
University of Nebraska-Lincoln Extension — Goumi Berry Production
